perturbation.original_text: During the Term, and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, the receiving party will not disclose to others or use for any purpose of its own, other than in performance of this Agreement, any Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, the receiving party will not disclose to others or use for any purpose of its own, other than in performance of this Agreement, any Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the confidentiality period to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that protect confidential information for longer periods if it maintains its secrecy and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures taken to maintain confidentiality as required by the law, especially considering the agreement involves technology and business strategies.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both indicate this could contradict California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets indefinitely if they remain confidential and provide economic value. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426, confirms the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act within California law. The shortened period could be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, violating the requirement to maintain confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of California without reference to its choice of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware without reference to its choice of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware might conflict with California law if a substantial portion of the contract performance occurs in California or if one party is primarily located in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.10
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.10 allows California courts to exercise jurisdiction over non-residents as long as it is consistent with the state and federal constitutions. Even if the agreement specifies Delaware law, California courts may still assert jurisdiction and apply California law if there are sufficient minimum contacts with the state, thus potentially overriding the choice of Delaware law specified in the contract, especially given that Snap Technologies, Inc. is a California corporation with its principal place of business in San Francisco.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law instead of California law, creating a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that despite this choice of law, California courts may still assert jurisdiction if there are sufficient minimum contacts with the state, particularly given the company's location. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 410.10, confirms that California courts can exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the state or federal constitutions. Therefore, the change directly introduces a conflict, since California law could still apply regardless of the contract stating Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate the Term upon not less than sixty (60) days prior written notice to the other party of any breach hereof by such other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate the Term upon not less than ten (10) days prior written notice to the other party of any breach hereof by such other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination due to breach to 10 days may be considered unreasonably short, especially for complex commercial agreements, and a court might find it unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2309 requires that termination of a contract, except on the happening of an agreed event, requires 'reasonable notification' be received by the other party. A ten-day notice period may be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature of the breach, the complexity of the agreement, and the potential harm caused by the termination. This is particularly true for co-branding agreements like the one presented, which often involve significant investment and ongoing collaborative efforts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by reducing the termination notice period from 60 to 10 days, likely creates a legal contradiction under California Commercial Code Section 2309. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest that a 10-day notice may be deemed unreasonable, especially in complex agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically COM 2309(3), states that termination requires 'reasonable notification' be received by the other party. The modified text's reduced notification period directly conflicts with this requirement, as a court could reasonably find 10 days to be insufficient, thus making the contractual term unenforceable. The original 60-day period is reduced to 10, significantly weakening the original legal intent to provide adequate notice for termination.
